mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive coverage from Stone Age lithics through Bronze and Iron Age metallurgy (C.1.a), ancient Mesopotamian and Greco-Roman engineering (C.1.b, C.1.c), medieval and Renaissance innovations (C.2), Industrial Revolution (C.3), and 20th-century computing, nuclear, and space technology (C.4), directly mapping to the full history of technology.

The transition from agrarian hand-production to mechanized factory manufacturing (1760-1840) corresponds to advances in power technology including steam power (C.3.a) and development of iron and steel, textiles, and transportation industries (C.3.b).
Foundational to general conceptions of technology (A) and the earliest technological development through stone, bone, and wood tools (C.1.a).
Energy conversion from chemical and thermal to mechanical forms corresponds to development of steam power and internal-combustion engines (C.3.a).
The ICE using Otto and diesel cycles directly corresponds to the development of the internal-combustion engine during the Industrial Revolution (C.3.a).
Pioneered practical electric power distribution and the incandescent light bulb, contributing to electric power exploitation (C.3.a) and development of communications industries (C.3.b).
Founded theoretical computer science via the Turing machine, directly contributing to automation and the electronic digital computer in Space Age technology (C.4.b).
Pioneered mass automobile production through the moving assembly line, corresponding to rationalization of production (C.4.a) and development of transportation industries (C.3.b).

The shift to information-technology-centered economies initiated by the transistor, internet, and World Wide Web corresponds to Space Age technology including automation and the electronic digital computer (C.4.b).
The earliest chemical explosive connects to technology and war (B.2) and medieval technological advances (C.2.a) as it spread through the Islamic world and Europe.
Firearms as projectile weapons propelled by chemical propellant directly relate to technology and war (B.2).
Machines as thermodynamic systems applying forces to perform work are central to general conceptions of technology (A) and advances in power technology (C.3.a).
Self-propelled vehicles for space exploration directly correspond to rocketry and space exploration in Space Age technology (C.4.b).
Greek technological achievements including philosophy, mathematics, and mechanical contrivances correspond to the mastery of iron and architectural works (C.1.c).
Roman engineering including roads, aqueducts, and architectural works corresponds to technological achievements of Greece and Rome (C.1.c).
Song dynasty polymath who recorded the magnetic compass, movable-type printing, and canal pound locks, corresponding to medieval technological advances (C.2.a).
The earliest known civilization with urban manufacturing, waterworks for irrigation, and cuneiform writing corresponds to the urban revolution (C.1.b).
Prehistoric lithic tool use from Oldowan tools through Neolithic agriculture corresponds to the beginnings of technology (C.1.a).
Design and operation of robots integrating AI and mechanical structures corresponds to automation in Space Age technology (C.4.b).
Medieval Islamic advances in mathematics, engineering, agriculture, and commerce correspond to medieval technological advances (C.2.a).
The relationship between systematic knowledge-building and technology relates to technology and education (B.3) as science informs and is informed by technological development.
Architecture as both visual art and applied engineering discipline relates to technology and art (B.4).
The transmission of knowledge through formal and informal processes relates to technology and education (B.3).
The relationship between art and technological development relates to technology and art (B.4).
Applied practice using science and mathematics to solve technological problems is central to general conceptions of technology (A).
The period 1500-1800 with scientific instruments, birth of steam power, and agricultural techniques corresponds to the emergence of Western technology (C.2.b).
The automobile as a major transportation technology connects to the development of transportation industries (C.3.b).
Bronze smelting and use corresponds to the beginnings of technology with early metal use (C.1.a) and the urban revolution enabled by bronze tools and weapons (C.1.b).
The period 500-1500 CE with Islamic Golden Age innovations, gunpowder, and inter-civilizational trade networks corresponds to medieval technological advances (C.2.a).
